llm_result.summary: The case presents reliable sleep onset but early-morning awakening around 04:30 with rapid cognitive arousal, doomscrolling after awakening, and same-day daytime impairment. The proposed levers span BIO (circadian alignment, sleep environment, daytime energy), PSYCHO (arousal regulation, cognitive-behavioral strategies), and SOCIAL (digital environment management) to produce modular, measurable shifts in C01–C06. Each lever is atomic (targets a specific predictor/criteria) and modifiable without pharmacology or unsafe practices. Implementation can be tracked with simple sleep/activity diaries, objective wearables where available, and targeted behavior logs over a 2–4 week horizon."

solutions.predictor_variable: Morning light exposure and fixed wake time for circadian entrainment
solutions.solution_candidate: Establish a fixed wake time (e.g., 06:30) and implement a brief morning light exposure within 15-30 minutes of waking (prefer bright light or outdoor exposure ~10–20 minutes). Gradually shift bedtime earlier by 15–30 minutes to achieve 7–8 hours of sleep. Maintain consistent weekend times.
solutions.mechanism_rationale: Regularized wake times and morning light cue strengthen circadian stability, reducing nocturnal awakenings and premature arousal at ~04:30 by aligning sleep pressure and melatonin rhythm.
solutions.measurement: Sleep diary entries for wake time consistency; actigraphy-derived sleep maintenance metrics; morning energy/mood rating.
solutions.safety_notes: Monitor for light sensitivity or sleep-onset issues if bedtime is moved too early.

solutions.predictor_variable: In-bed device-free wind-down and pre-sleep routine
solutions.solution_candidate: Implement a strict 60-minute device-free pre-sleep window with a consistent wind-down (e.g., reading, breathing exercises, journaling). Remove devices from the bed and use blue-light filters when needed. Establish a ritual that signals sleep readiness without stimulating content.
solutions.mechanism_rationale: Reducing bed-time cognitive engagement prevents activation and supports relaxation, lowering the likelihood of arousal-driven awakenings.
solutions.measurement: In-bed device usage logs; adherence to pre-sleep routine; subjective calm before intended sleep; time to fall back asleep after awakening.
solutions.safety_notes: Ensure accessible alternative activities for stress management; avoid isolation.

solutions.predictor_variable: Digital content boundaries to reduce doomscroll exposure
solutions.solution_candidate: Create a digital boundary plan: schedule doomscroll windows (e.g., 2x daily for 10–15 minutes) and curate content to reduce negative reactivity; incorporate adaptive coping strategies (breathing, grounding) during these windows.
solutions.mechanism_rationale: Limiting exposure to distressing content reduces overnight cognitive activation and negative affect, decreasing morning arousal and rumination.
solutions.measurement: Minutes spent doomscrolling; mood and anxiety ratings after wakeups; diary entries on content type.
solutions.safety_notes: Escalate if distress worsens; maintain social connection in healthy ways.

solutions.predictor_variable: Awakening arousal management with brief breathing/grounding
solutions.solution_candidate: Upon awakening (~04:30), perform 4–6 cycles of paced breathing (about 4–6 breaths per minute) plus a 60–90 second grounding exercise; if sleep does not resume within 15–20 minutes, rise calmly and engage in a quiet, non-stimulating activity until sleep pressure returns.
solutions.mechanism_rationale: Immediate autonomic downregulation and cognitive grounding reduce racing thoughts and facilitate either a smoother return to sleep or a restful wakeful period.
solutions.measurement: Subjective arousal rating on waking; time to fall back asleep after awakening; sleep diary latency after awakening.

solutions.predictor_variable: Structured daytime activity to reduce daytime impairment (C06)
solutions.solution_candidate: Introduce a daily, scheduled 15–20 minute moderate-intensity activity (e.g., brisk walk) in the early afternoon with 10–20 minutes of outdoor light exposure, consistent hydration and meals to support energy regulation and daytime functioning.
solutions.mechanism_rationale: Enhanced daytime energy and neurophysiological regulation improve daytime functioning and reduce negative cognitive spillover into the night.
solutions.measurement: Midday energy/alertness ratings; daily activity log or step count; subjective daytime functioning scores.
solutions.safety_notes: Consult clinician if medical conditions limit activity.
